coredata.dc:title: Lithium salts as active corrosion inhibitors for aluminum substrates
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Bergseth, Zachary
Qi, Xiaoning
Upadhyay, Vinod
Battocchi, Dante
coredata.dc:description: As an effort to replace carcinogenic chromate from protective coatings, researchers are continuously searching and proposing new non-toxic chemicals sharing similar properties: leachable, responsive, and film-forming. Lithium (Li), in the form of either a salt additive in coatings or alloyed with aluminum (Al), is observed to be able to form insoluble protective layers and thus reduce the corrosion rate of the Al substrates. The protective film formed is often composed of Li, Al, carbonate (CO3), hydroxide (OH) etc., sharing similar structure to a layered double hydroxide (LDH) as indicated by several mechanistic studies. The successful formation of the irreversible/insoluble protective film requires oxygen, correct pH, and the right combination of available ion species. In this study, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results show that these leachable inhibitor coatings yield poor barrier properties before and after artificial weathering. Scanning electron microscopy pictures show the growth of an inorganic layer between the coating and aluminum substrate, indicative of the formation of a layered double hydroxide (LDH) structure. Current mapping generated by scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET) indicates that aerated lithium salt solutions can cause pitting corrosion over bare aluminum, while organic coatings pigmented with lithium were able to keep the surface relatively passive, with and without the presence of a defect. An anodic shift of the surface of the organic coatings pigmented with lithium containing a defect is seen while keeping the surface relatively passive, indicating anodic passivation of the metal surface.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bode modulus and phase angle plots of a control coating (MgO and TiO2), and a coating pigmented with lithium salt. The measurement area is 7.1 cm2.
Bode modulus and phase angle plots of the lithium oxalate salt filled coating during salt spray exposure (ASTM B117). The measurement area is 7.1 cm2.
Bode modulus and phase angle plots of a lithium oxalate salt filled coating during constant immersion in 3.5% NaCl. The measurement area is 7.1 cm2.

Morphology and elemental analysis with field-emmision scanning electron microsope (FESEM) a.) before B117 exposure; b.) afer B117 exposure; c.) elemental linescan location; d.) elemental disribution along the linescan.

Localized anodic and cathodic sites mapped by scanning vibrating electrode techniques (SVET). Plots on top are first mappings at 0 hour, in middle are the 12 hours, and at bottom are the 24 hours mappings. Left side (a-c) is with 0.1% NaCl solution and right side (d-f) with 0.1% NaCl and 0.1% Li2CO3.

Localized anodic and cathodic sites mapped by SVET of an epoxy pigmented with lithium salts. Plots on top are initial mappings, in middle are the 2 hours’, and at bottom are the 4 hours mappings. Left side is the intact coating (a-c) in 0.1% NaCl solution and right side (d-f) the same coating with a 0.5mm defect in 0.1% NaCl.

head.ce:title: Lithium salts as active corrosion inhibitors for aluminum substrates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Coatings and Polymeric Materials, North Dakota State University, Fargo, ND 58102, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Coatings and Polymeric Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Coatings and Polymeric Materials, North Dakota State University, Fargo, ND 58102, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an effort to replace carcinogenic chromate from protective coatings, researchers are continuously searching and proposing new non-toxic chemicals sharing similar properties: leachable, responsive, and film-forming. Lithium (Li), in the form of either a salt additive in coatings or alloyed with aluminum (Al), is observed to be able to form insoluble protective layers and thus reduce the corrosion rate of the Al substrates. The protective film formed is often composed of Li, Al, carbonate (CO3), hydroxide (OH) etc., sharing similar structure to a layered double hydroxide (LDH) as indicated by several mechanistic studies. The successful formation of the irreversible/insoluble protective film requires oxygen, correct pH, and the right combination of available ion species. In this study, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results show that these leachable inhibitor coatings yield poor barrier properties before and after artificial weathering. Scanning electron microscopy pictures show the growth of an inorganic layer between the coating and aluminum substrate, indicative of the formation of a layered double hydroxide (LDH) structure. Current mapping generated by scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET) indicates that aerated lithium salt solutions can cause pitting corrosion over bare aluminum, while organic coatings pigmented with lithium were able to keep the surface relatively passive, with and without the presence of a defect. An anodic shift of the surface of the organic coatings pigmented with lithium containing a defect is seen while keeping the surface relatively passive, indicating anodic passivation of the metal surface.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Generally used in the form of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), chromate has been used for over a century, ranging from wood treatments to corrosion protection. Chromate pigment does such a great job at being a corrosion inhibitive pigment that it has become a benchmark for corrosion protection. When exposed to a corrosive environment, hexavalent chromium is reduced to trivalent chromium, forming a corrosion-preventing passivation layer on the surface, which is stable over a wide range of pH. Chromate works so well due to its dual ability to act as an anodic and cathodic inhibitor where it restricts the rate of metal dissolution and reduction reactions from both oxygen and water. The chromate ion (CrO4 2−) leaches into the electrolyte and out to the active sites where cathodic reduction occurs to form Cr2O3 chromium oxide film. This oxide film is very resilient: it is relatively insoluble in aqueous environments across a large range of pH, and it adheres to the underlying metal strongly. However, being a carcinogenic chemical with rising environmental concerns, chromate is now being phased out and researchers are continuously searching and proposing new non-toxic chemicals sharing similar properties: leachable, responsive, and film-forming [1,2]. Lithium (Li), in the form or either salt additive in coatings or alloyed with aluminum (Al), is observed to be able to form insoluble protective layers and thus reduce the corrosion rate of the Al substrates [3]. The proposed protective film that is formed is a layered double hydroxide (LDH) composed of Li, Al, carbonate (CO3), hydroxide (OH) etc., as indicated by several mechanistic studies [3–10]. The successful formation of the irreversible/insoluble protective film requires oxygen, correct pH, and the right combination of available ion species. There have been studies of lithium salts as primer coating additives on their film formation mechanisms and compositions [3].

The effects of lithium on the corrosion of aluminum were first investigated with aluminum-lithium alloys, in which lithium can leach out and change the electrolyte that governs the corrosion/protection mechanisms of the alloy. Craig, et al, reported that the alloy shavings from an Al-Li-Cu-Mg alloy (8090-T651) increased pH of 3.5% NaCl solution without air contact [11]. The study from Gui et al showed that anodic passivation is observed using Li2CO3 but not with Na2CO3 and K2CO3 at higher pH (around 11.5) [6]. Rangel et al reported aeration of the Li2CO3 solution makes the open circuit potential shifted to more cathodic direction [12]. There was also an effort on forming a protective layer by exposing aluminum to lithium containing solution, which can be used as a pretreatment process [4]. In Buchheit's work, the formation of an irreversible layered hydrotalcites was confirmed by XRD and the film improved corrosion resistance of several aluminum alloys. These studies investigate direct interaction between Li metal/salts and Al metal, providing insight on critical requirements for Li salts to be beneficial. For example, the observed higher pH indicates Li salts are more likely to inhibit cathodic reactions.

Besides earlier investigation on their positive effect when alloyed with aluminum, lithium salts have been studied as pigments in organic coatings. Drewin et al. coated Al 1100 coupons through the formation of a lithium-aluminum-carbonate-hydroxide hydrate, precipitated out of 0.1M lithium carbonate alkaline solution [13]. Liu et al. and Visser et al. have pigmented polyurethane and epoxy coatings with lithium oxalate and lithium carbamate, where they observed leachable behavior of the lithium salts into a scribe after exposure to continuous salt spray (ASTM B117), and demonstrated the formation of a protective layer containing lithium [8,9,14,15]. Marcoen et al. pigmented a polyurethane coating with lithium carbonate. After the coating being exposed to ASTM B117 salt spray, they found a lithium-containing protective LDH layer inside of the scribe and mapped the growth stages of the protective layer [3]. Trentin et al. investigated the role of lithium salts in silica coatings, by pigmenting a PMMA silica coating with lithium carbonate. Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) they found that increasing lithium carbonate would increase the corrosion resistance and yielded high coating impedance. In addition, through results of time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) and SEM coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) they were able to show that the lithium leached out and participated in forming the protective layer [5].

Lithium salts are reported to have promising anti-corrosive properties both as an additive or a treatment solution for substrates of aluminum alloys. However, adding soluble salts into a coating system always raises the concern of creating artificial coating defects. Here we report efforts on characterizing 1) direct interaction between lithium salt solution and an Al substrate, 2) interaction between Li-salt-filled coating and the Al substrate, and 3) coatings’ impedance change during their exposure to salt-spray and constant immersion. Both general EIS and localized electrochemical techniques (SVET) are used to characterize the effects of lithium salts solution on a Al substrate and as a filler in an epoxy coating system.

Lithium salts used in this study are lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) and lithium oxalate (Li2C2O4) from Sigma-Aldrich. Coating pigments are magnesium oxide (MgO) from Sigma-Aldrich and titanium oxide R-960 (TiO2) from Dupont. Xylene as the solvent was from VWR. The epoxy resin Epon 828 and curing agent Epikure 3164 were purchased from Hexion. Aluminum substrates were Q-panel A-36 Aluminum H14 Mill Finish and Aluminum alloy 2024 T3 (used for SVET), and all the substrates were used as received.

To disperse the MgO and TiO2, a grind base with Epon 828 and both pigments were mixed under high shear and high speed using a Dispermat high-speed disperser at 10000 RPM for 20 minutes. The grind base was then added into individual formula to make the part A of the liquid formula. Before mixing part A and part B, curing agent Epikure 3164 was diluted with xylene. The stoichiometry of part A and part B was controlled at 1:1. Detailed formulas are shown in Table 1 . Li salts were milled with a mechanical grinder and sieved with a 25µm sieve. Part A with Li salts were also filtered to remove a few large clusters that may cause coating defects.
Liquid formulas were applied using a drawdown bar with 102 microns wet film thickness. Applied films were dried in an oven at 80°C for 20 minutes.

Samples were exposed to two weathering protocols: continuous salt spray (ASTM B117) and constant immersion in 3.5% sodium chloride aqueous solution for over 1500 hours. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to characterize the coating barrier performance during the weathering process, with panels being washed with DI water, then dried before testing.

EIS spectra were measured using a Gamry Reference 600 potentiostat before and after weathering/immersion. The solution used for the EIS cell was 3.5% (w/w) sodium chloride (NaCl) aqueous solution. EIS measurement frequencies are from 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz. A sinusoidal perturbation was set to 10 mV versus open circuit potential of the coated samples. The measurement area is 7.1 cm2. EIS cell setup is shown in Fig. 1 . The cell comprises a glass cylinder cell being clamped and sealed on top of the samples with an O-ring. Both the reference electrode (RE) and counter electrode (CE) were situated in the glass cell filled with 3.5% NaCl solution. Aluminum substrate was the working electrode (WE) connected to corresponding lead. The reference electrode used in this study is a saturated calomel electrode (SCE), while the counter electrode is a niobium-coated stainless-steel mesh. The reference electrode was immersed in the testing solution (3.5% NaCl) for 10 minutes before any measurement.

Scanning electron microscope (JEOL JSM-7600F field-emission SEM) was used to characterize the morphology and elemental composition of a cross-section of the coatings. JEOL IB09010CP Cross Section Polisher was used to polish the cross-section, and each sample surface was coated with carbon before being analyzed by the SEM.

Scanning Vibrating Electrode Technique (SVET), instrument from Applicable Electronics, Inc, was used to analyze the effects of different salts on the localized corrosion of the aluminum substrate, and to observe how the current density of a lithium pigmented coating system is affected over time with and without an artificial defect. The SVET probe is a platinum iridium microelectrode (250 μm x 1” with a tip diameter of 1-2 μm) purchased from microprobes.com. The probe tip is platinized using a platinum chloride plating solution to make the tip of the insulated probe conductive. The probe was placed and vibrated at 200 microns above the surface under investigation. An area of 6 × 6 mm was scanned on the Al substrates. The set of SVET data on bare Al 2024T3 (polished with 2000 grit sandpaper) was obtained from scans every 4 hours for a total of 24 hours. The set of data on samples with coatings containing Li salts was obtained from scans every 30 minutes for a total of 4 hours. For coated samples, both intact coatings and coatings with centered artificial defects are characterized by SVET. The artificial defect was created with a 0.5 mm drill bit.

SEM/EDS analyses show that an inorganic layer was formed at the interface of the coating and the Al substrate, composed of mainly aluminum, oxygen, and some carbon elements (Fig. 5 d). This layer was not present at the coating/substrate interface before B117 salt spray exposure (Fig. 5a). The layer's showed columnar morphology and as a secondary layer which was also observed by Marcoen et al on scribed areas of coated samples (bare aluminum adjacent to a Li-filled coating film). The SEM/EDS results clearly showed the growth of a columnar inorganic layer that replaces the coating/substrate interface during the salt spray exposure. SEM/EDS cannot detect lithium element and leave the potential chemical structure of the layer undetermined. However, in combination with literature findings, one can hypothesize that this layer could be the formation of a lithium LDH layer between the coating and substrate, like how the LDH layer is formed inside of scribed areas as seen in published studies [8,9,14,15]. Visser et al reported the formation of an inorganic layer. To confirm the presence of Li, Electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) could be used [3,9,14,16].

Literature reports Li salts as a potential inhibitor in organic coatings. However, the potential detrimental effects of a soluble salt to a coating are often overlooked and should be further investigated. When bare aluminum (2024 T3) substrate was exposed to 0.1% NaCl (solution in contact with air, Figs. 6 a-c), localized anodic and cathodic sites are shown with minimum current density across 24 hours of exposure. At time 0 hour, both solutions have relatively passive surfaces, where the 0.1% Li2CO3 solution (d-f) shows a small anodic and cathodic site forming at the corner. This can be signs of pitting corrosion beginning to occur already, raising the concern of detrimental effects of localized higher concentration of Li salt, which are likely due the heterogeneous nature of a coating formula and would be very difficult to detect without removing the coating. After 12 hours, both solutions form a relatively large anode at the corners of their respective scans, indicating that pitting corrosion is continuing. After 24 hours, the 0.1% NaCl solution has suppressed the anodic spike and yields another relatively passive surface, likely a result of the formation of the aluminum oxide barrier. The 0.1% Li2CO3 solution has suppressed and passivated most of the surface, while the corner still yields the localized anodic and cathodic site as seen at time 0 hour. These observations are in accordance with other researchers’ findings on aluminum alloys’ susceptibility to corrosion when exposed to aerated lithium salt solution [12]. In this study, dispersed Li salt particles are surrounded by coating matrix, which would slow oxygen diffusion and limit aeration of the Li salt solution in contact with the aluminum alloy substrate, resulting in a favorable inhibitive environment. Soluble salts usually are detrimental to the performance of a protective coating. For the inhibitive effects of a Li salt to outweigh its detrimental effects, there should exist an optimum Li salt load that provides a balance of the coating barrier performance and sufficient leachable Li salt for inhibition.

In the case of a lithium pigmented coating (Fig. 7 ), SVET scans over intact coating (a-c) and with a 0.5mm circular artificial defect (d-f) shows a relatively inactive surface over the 4-hour immersion in 0.1% NaCl (w/w). Scan time was reduced to 4 hours, since it has been shown that the lithium LDH can form as fast as 15 minutes inside a scribe [3]. At the start of the initial scan, a local anode and cathode can be seen in the middle of the coated sample. This is likely a result of the heterogeneous nature of lithium salt dispersion in a polymer-based coating matrix. Locations with higher Li salt concentration (the proximity of a salt particle) are more readily to have Li ion diffuse into the solution. After 2 hours of immersion both the anodic and cathodic site have disappeared, and the intact coating forms a passive surface across the scanned area, likely caused by the formation of the LDH layer. This observation persists over the 4-hour scan; however, several small cathodic sites and an anodic site appear to form. This could be caused by the breakdown of the LDH layer, or from areas where lithium salt has diffused out into solution but was not converted into an LDH layer due to the specific formation conditions not being met. Another possibility is that the local oxygen depletion resulting from the LDH formation.

A closer look at the lithium salt pigmented coating with a defect (Figs. 7, d-f) reveals that the surface of the coating is very active, with larger anodic and cathodic regions around the edge of the scan. After 2 hours, the overall OCP of the system increases, in agreement with the findings of Gui et al that the system start to form anodic passivation [6]. This effect persists through the 4 hours immersion scan, and there are no signs of local anodes or cathodes inside of the defect where exposed substrate is present. The defect area showed no signs of corrosion products, likely due to the formation of the thin protective inorganic layer, or anodic passivation, however, the entire surface becomes more active over time. As discussed earlier, the effects of soluble salt in a coating could potentially outweighs the benefits the formation of the inorganic protective layer, especially in the later stages of the coating's service, when the leachable Li salt is depleted.

Coatings were modified with lithium salts to investigate their potential to be used as a new non-toxic additive to replace carcinogenic chromate. It was found at the load of 16.5% of lithium oxalate and 19% lithium carbonate, these coatings have very low barrier performance indicated by the lower low-frequency impedance moduli. During exposure to salt spray, there is a layer formed at the coating/substrate interface composed of carbon, oxygen, aluminum. When lithium salt solution is directly in contact with bare aluminum substrate, the substrate appears more prone to pitting corrosion. SVET scans were able to confirm that aerated lithium salt solutions can cause pitting corrosion, while pigmented lithium organic coatings were able to keep the coating surface relatively passive, with and without the presence of the artificial defect. When the artificial defect is present, an anodic shift as an indication of passivation, was observed on the surface with the coating containing a lithium salt. The finding of the active behaviors when Li salt solution and bare Al substrate in direct contact imply local high Li ion concentration could lead to under-film pitting corrosion, which needs further systematic investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this manuscript.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of Alternatives to chromate for corrosion protection of aluminum aerospace alloys
Prog. Org. Coat.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Twite, R. L.; Bierwagen, G. P. Review of Alternatives to chromate for corrosion protection of aluminum aerospace alloys. Prog. Org. Coat. 1998, 33, 91–100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chromate replacement: what does the future hold?
npj Mater. Degrad.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gharbi, O.; Thomas, S.; Smith, C.; Birbilis, N. Chromate replacement: what does the future hold? npj Mater. Degrad. 2018, 2 (1). DOI: 10.1038/s41529-018-0034-5.

Compositional study of a corrosion protective layer formed by leachable lithium salts in a coating defect on AA2024-T3 aluminium alloys. Prog. Org. Coat. 2018, 119, 65–75. DOI: 10.1016/j.porgcoat.2018.02.011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The corrosion protection of AA2024-T3 aluminium alloy by leaching of lithium-containing salts from organic coatings
Farad. Discuss.
